title: Impersonation Attacks
description: Identify impersonation attempts and verify senders.

questions.question_text: An email claims to be from your CEO requesting an urgent transfer. What is the safest action?
questions.explanation: Impersonation and urgency are common. Verify through a separate channel.

answers.answer_text: Verify the request through a known channel

questions.question_text: Which indicator suggests impersonation?
questions.explanation: Display names can be spoofed; check the actual sender address.

answers.answer_text: Reply-to domain differs from the organization

questions.question_text: A vendor asks to change bank details via email. What should you do?
questions.explanation: Verify through a known official contact.

answers.answer_text: Call the vendor using a known number
Forward to the team for votes

questions.question_text: Why are impersonation emails dangerous?
questions.explanation: They exploit trust in known identities.

answers.answer_text: They exploit trust in known identities

title: Typosquatting and Punycode
description: Spot lookalike domains and deceptive links.

questions.question_text: Which URL is most suspicious?
questions.explanation: Typosquatting uses lookalike characters or misspellings.

questions.question_text: What is a key risk of punycode domains?
questions.explanation: They can look identical to trusted domains.

answers.answer_text: They appear identical to trusted domains

questions.question_text: How should you validate a suspicious link?
questions.explanation: Compare the exact domain against a trusted source.

answers.answer_text: Click and see where it goes

questions.question_text: Typosquatting often relies on:
questions.explanation: Attackers swap characters or add extra letters.

title: External Sender Domains
description: Recognize when senders are outside your organization.

questions.question_text: Why is an external sender banner useful?
questions.explanation: It indicates the message came from outside your organization.

answers.answer_text: It indicates the email is external

questions.question_text: A vendor email arrives from a free email domain. What should you do?
questions.explanation: Verify the sender using a known contact method.

questions.question_text: Which is a red flag for external domains?
questions.explanation: Similar-looking but different domains indicate risk.

answers.answer_text: Domain is similar but not identical

questions.question_text: How should you handle unexpected external requests?
questions.explanation: Confirm legitimacy through trusted channels.

title: Spoofing
description: Learn how spoofed sender identities are used.

questions.question_text: What is a common sign of spoofing?
questions.explanation: Authentication checks may fail even if the sender looks familiar.

questions.question_text: Which header helps reveal spoofing?
questions.explanation: Received headers show the true path of the email.

questions.question_text: If an email fails DMARC, what should you do?
questions.explanation: Treat it as suspicious and verify the request.

questions.question_text: Spoofing attempts often rely on:
questions.explanation: They forge sender identity to gain trust.

title: Social Engineering Text
description: Understand manipulative language and pressure tactics.

questions.question_text: Why do attackers use emotional language?
questions.explanation: It pushes people to act quickly and emotionally.

questions.question_text: A message says Do this now or lose access. This is an example of:
questions.explanation: This is a classic urgency tactic.

questions.question_text: What is the safest response to a pressure-based request?
questions.explanation: Pause and verify independently.

questions.question_text: Social engineering attacks primarily target:
questions.explanation: They exploit human behavior and trust.

questions.question_text: Why do attackers use urgent language?
questions.explanation: Urgency pressures people to act without verifying.

questions.question_text: What is a safe response to urgent requests?
questions.explanation: Verify first through trusted channels.

questions.question_text: Urgent payment requests are often linked to:
questions.explanation: They can indicate business email compromise.

questions.question_text: When should you respond to urgent requests?
questions.explanation: After verifying legitimacy.

title: Fake Invoice
description: Detect invoice fraud and payment scams.

questions.question_text: A surprise invoice arrives from an unknown vendor. What should you do first?
questions.explanation: Confirm the vendor and order history through official channels.

answers.answer_text: Verify the vendor and order history

questions.question_text: Which is a red flag in invoice emails?
questions.explanation: Mismatched sender domains are a common sign of fraud.

questions.question_text: What is the safest way to pay invoices?
questions.explanation: Use approved payment workflows.

questions.question_text: If an invoice includes a QR code to pay, what should you do?
questions.explanation: Verify the source and destination before scanning.

questions.question_text: Why is a file named invoice.pdf.exe suspicious?
questions.explanation: The final extension indicates it is an executable, not a PDF.

questions.question_text: What should you do with unexpected attachments?
questions.explanation: Verify the sender and the request before opening.

answers.answer_text: Verify the request through a known channel

questions.question_text: Why are macro-enabled files risky?
questions.explanation: Macros can execute hidden code.

questions.question_text: Which is the safest policy for attachments?
questions.explanation: Scan and verify before opening attachments.

questions.question_text: What is a common sign of a fake login page?
questions.explanation: The domain does not exactly match the real service.

questions.question_text: How should you access a login page safely?
questions.explanation: Use a trusted bookmark or type the known URL.

answers.answer_text: Use a trusted bookmark or type the URL

questions.question_text: Why doesn’t the padlock icon guarantee a login page is safe?
questions.explanation: HTTPS only encrypts the connection; it doesn’t prove legitimacy.

answers.answer_text: It always means the site is fake
It only means the connection is encrypted

questions.question_text: A login page asks for your password and MFA code on a site you reached from an email link. What should you do?
questions.explanation: Navigate directly to the official site instead.

answers.answer_text: Close it and navigate to the official site

questions.question_text: Why is it risky when someone asks to continue the conversation on personal SMS or WhatsApp?
questions.explanation: It bypasses normal controls and verification channels.

answers.answer_text: It avoids official oversight and verification

questions.question_text: What is the safest response to a request to move to a personal channel?
questions.explanation: Keep communication on approved channels.

questions.question_text: A vendor asks you to confirm a payment change via text. What should you do?
questions.explanation: Verify through an official, known contact.

answers.answer_text: Verify via a known official contact

questions.question_text: Side channel requests are often a sign of:
questions.explanation: Attackers try to move you away from monitored channels.

questions.question_text: What is the main risk of adversary-in-the-middle phishing kits like Evilginx?
questions.explanation: They can capture session tokens after you log in, even if you used MFA.

answers.answer_text: They capture session tokens after login

questions.question_text: Why can proxy phishing sometimes bypass MFA?
questions.explanation: The attacker proxies the real login and captures the authenticated session.

answers.answer_text: The attacker steals the session after MFA succeeds
Passwords are stored in the browser

questions.question_text: Which action best reduces proxy phishing risk?
questions.explanation: Use phishing-resistant MFA and verify the exact domain before logging in.

questions.question_text: A login page looks identical to a real service but uses a lookalike domain. What should you do?
questions.explanation: HTTPS and design can be spoofed; always verify the exact domain.

answers.answer_text: Trust it because it looks real
Verify the exact domain before logging in
Ignore only if the logo is wrong

title: Phishing Awareness Fundamentals
description: Test your ability to identify phishing attempts, suspicious emails, and social engineering tactics.

questions.question_text: You receive an email asking you to confirm account details via a link. What should you do?
questions.explanation: Use the official site or contact method, not the email link.

answers.answer_text: Use the official website or contact method

questions.question_text: Which URL is most likely phishing?
questions.explanation: Subdomains can make links look legitimate.

questions.question_text: What tactic is used in “act now or lose access” emails?
questions.explanation: This is a classic urgency tactic.

questions.question_text: An email from a manager asks for gift cards. What should you do?
questions.explanation: Verify unusual requests through another channel.

questions.question_text: What is the safest way to check a link?
questions.explanation: Hover to preview and verify the domain.

answers.answer_text: Click to see where it goes
Copy into a new tab without checking

questions.question_text: Which header detail helps detect spoofing?
questions.explanation: Authentication results and Received headers show origin.

questions.question_text: Why is a file named “report.pdf.exe” suspicious?
questions.explanation: The last extension indicates an executable file.

questions.question_text: A text message says your delivery failed with a shortened link. This is:
questions.explanation: Smishing uses SMS to trick users.

questions.question_text: What makes spear phishing more dangerous?
questions.explanation: It targets individuals with personalized information.

questions.question_text: Why does MFA help protect against phishing?
questions.explanation: It adds an extra factor beyond the password.

title: Advanced Phishing Tactics
description: Master the sophisticated techniques used by modern threat actors.

questions.question_text: You receive an email with no links or attachments, but it contains a tiny, 1x1 transparent image. What is its likely purpose?
questions.explanation: Pixel tracking allows attackers to verify your email is active and collect data like your IP address and device type.

answers.answer_text: To 'fingerprint' your device and track when you open the email
It is a technical error in the email client

questions.question_text: A suspicious email subject line begins with 'RE:' or 'FW:', appearing to be part of a past conversation. Why is this a red flag?
questions.explanation: Thread hijacking exploits psychological trust by making a malicious email look like a continuation of a legitimate thread.

answers.answer_text: It means the email was definitely sent by a colleague
It is a tactic to bypass your initial skepticism through false familiarity
It indicates the email is a formal corporate announcement
The sender is replying to a message you never sent

questions.question_text: Why might an advanced phisher include a small, subtle typo (like 'Microsof') only in the legal disclaimer at the bottom of a perfect email?
questions.explanation: Modern phishers avoid obvious errors in the pitch but use 'professional' flaws to make the email seem more 'human' and less automated.

answers.answer_text: Because they have poor grammar skills
To make the email appear more legitimate and less like a bot-generated template
To avoid being caught by spell-checkers
It is a secret code for other attackers

questions.question_text: What makes 'Quishing' (QR Phishing) particularly dangerous compared to standard link-based phishing?
questions.explanation: QR codes often redirect users to mobile devices where security controls are weaker and URLs are harder to inspect.

answers.answer_text: QR codes can only be scanned by secure devices
They bypass many email scanners and move the attack to less-protected mobile devices
Scanning a QR code always installs a virus immediately
QR codes are harder for phishers to generate than links
